rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF DAVID FRANK PHILLIPS II
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 12/16/25 at Counsel's request in order to amend the proposed distribution again. (No earlier availability.) Additional issue(s): (1) The 11/23/22 order for preliminary distribution ordered distribution of the 50% interest in Amicus Associates Limited Partnership to Adam Phillips, as Trustee of the Arianna Legacy Trust. There is no mention of Chistina Lazaridi as a co-trustee. (2) The 11/23/22 order for preliminary distribution ordered the $1,000 gift to David L. Ghent, Jr., PLUS INTEREST AT THE LEGAL RATE STARTING MARCH 26, 2021. The first transfer of $300 was not made until 10/8/25. With the other two (incomplete?) transfers, the total distributed or attempted to be distributed is just $1,000.00. (3) One of the receipts signed by Samuel Noah Phillips is for just "Assignment of Copyright" without any indication of which copyright. (4) Jon Sneddon apparently received the philatelic collection in January 2022, although the preliminary distribution was not granted until 11/23/22. (5) Princeton's receipt for just the book collection signed on 8/27/25 does not reflect the condition that it will place the heraldic book collection in open stacks. (6) Not all receipts for the preliminary distributions ordered on 11/23/22 have been filed yet. (7) It is unclear how the executor determined that assets on hand have a value of $1,990,008.59; that is not what his accounting ending on 12/31/24 shows. (8) Paragraph 6 of the second amendment has conflicting amounts for the reserve being requested... $104,000 or $140,000? (9) The amounts paid to Christopher Phillips and to Rabbi Ungar for an honorarium and/or assistance in preparing the heraldic book total $33,000, not just $18,000. The proposed distribution set forth in the second amendment has not been reviewed, as it will reportedly be amended again. Deadline is 12/2/25 to provide the assigned examiner courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues. Please do not file another pleading form of proof service (they are meaningless); the only correct form of notice is the JC form DE-120, on the reverse side of which is the proof of service that should be completed. A supplement or a "declaration" that does not amend the petition does not need to be noticed. [CRC 7.53(b).] Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE IN RE THE ERIC AND JANE DIAZ TRUST, DATED AUGUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition For Confirmation Of Trust Assets (Probate Code Section 850 A 3
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 12/24/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) Prob. C. Sect. 850 is a shortcut for a formal probate proceeding. A beneficiary of the trust receives mailed notice with a copy of the petition, but any heir-at-law, who would stand to inherit, if the trust did not exist or were invalid, is a party whose right, title or interest would be affected by this petition, and, therefore, must receive (or waive) personal service of notice. [Prob. C. Sect. 17203(b) and CCP Sect. 413.10 et seq.] (2) Notice must have attached to it the procedures for how to make a remote appearance by Zoom and CourtCall, as well as remote public access, as set out at https://sf.courts.ca.gov/divisions/probate-court. [LRSF 14.1 C.6.] (3) A copy of each account statement immediately preceding the trustor's date of death must be filed to confirm how title to the accounts was vested. Please provide courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 12/10/25. After the supplement to the petition is e-filed, notice, with a copy of the petition and the supplement, must be given to the heirs-at-law (who are apparently also remainder beneficiaries under the trust). Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. (NB: This Court will not make an order validating a trust via an 850 petition. If the trust were not presumed to be valid, the petitioner should not be petitioning to confirm that it held assets. Nor will this Court include an omnibus clause confirming all subsequently discovered accounts to be assets of the trust.)

rulings.Case Title: ESTATE OF VICTORIA L. BRUGNARA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer Of Respondents To Petitioners Amended Petition Of Luke D. Brugnara; Demurrer; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Demurrer of Respondents to Petitioner's Amended Petition of Luke D. Brugnara. Per the Notice of Demurrer, Respondents demur generally to Petitioner Luke D. Brugnara's Petition to Invalidate the Amendments to Victoria L. Brugnara Revocable Trust Dated May 21, 1998, Removal of Trustees and for Damages Against Krista M. Brugnara Individually and as Trustee, Eric J. Brugnara Individually and as Trustee and Kurt D. Huysentruyt and Does 1 through 50 ("Amended Petition"), generally, on the grounds that the Amended Petition fails to state a cause of action and is barred by Probate Code section 16061.8 as untimely, and specifically as to each cause of action on the basis of lack of pleading ultimate facts rather than legal conclusions and contentions. Respondents' Request for Judicial Notice of exhibits A-1 through N, attached to both the request for Judicial Noice and to the declaration of Krista M. Brugnara, under the provisions of Evidence Code sections 452, 452.5, 453, and 454 is GRANTED, as required by Evid. Code section 453. The court notes Exhibits M and N appear to be offered to the court to impeach Petitioner's credibility (see, Demurrer at 16:21-24). A party's credibility is irrelevant for the purposes of a demurrer. The court also notes, judicial notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in those materials. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) "A court may take judicial notice of the existence of each document in a court file, but can only take judicial notice of the truth of facts asserted in documents such as orders, findings of fact and conclusions of law, and judgments. [citation.] [ ] A mistaken notion exists that taking judicial notice of court records means noticing " '... the existence of facts asserted in [all the documents in] a court file ... a court cannot take judicial notice of hearsay allegations as being true, just because they are part of a court record or file....' " (Garcia v. Sterling (1985) 176 Cal. App. 3d 17, 21-22, quoting Day v. Sharp (1975) 50 Cal.App.3d 904, 914, emphasis in original.) A demurrer is a pleading used to challenge the legal sufficiency of an opponent's pleading, based on defects that appear either on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (See Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318; County of Fresno v. Shelton (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th 996, 1008, 1009.) Here, the general demurrer is bought on the argument and proffered evidence that Petitioner was served notice pursuant to Probate Code section 16061.7, failed to timely contest the validity of the Trust pursuant to Probate Code section 16061.8, and is thus time-barred from bringing the instant Amended Petition. The Amended Petition pleads at paragraph 22, Petitioner, who was in federal custody from May 17, 2023 until August 12, 2024 never received nor knew of the document entitled Notification of Trustee Pursuant to Probate Code [sec.] 16061.7 dated 8/16/23 by Krista and Eric ("Notice"). Petitioner is informed and believes and thereon alleges that the Notice was not mailed to him. Alternatively, Krista and Eric did not make a reasonably diligent effort to send Notice to an address that was reasonably calculated to be received by petitioner, or to otherwise determine that Petitioner had received Notice. In fact, Petitioner never received Notice.

rulings.Case Title: ESTATE OF VICTORIA L. BRUGNARA
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For Purposes Of Posting Tentative Ruling.
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] When considering a demurrer, the trial court must accept as true all material facts pleaded in the complaint and those arising by reasonable implication. (Moore v. Conliffe (1994) 7 Cal. 4th 634, 638; Montclair Parkowners Ass'n v. City of Montclair (1999) 76 Cal. App. 4th 784.; Sheehan v. San Francisco 49ers, Ltd. (2009) Cal.4th 992, 998.) The sole question raised by demurrer is whether the pleading is facially-not factually-deficient. Demurrers test legal sufficiency based on the allegations as pleaded, without regard to the plaintiff's ability to prove the alleged facts. (see, Gray v. Dignity Health (2021) 70 Cal.App.5th 225.) Here, the Amended Petition facially pleads around the Probate Code section 16061.8 statute of limitation, stating Petitioner did not receive notice served pursuant to Probate Code section 16061.7. Respondents contest that factual assertion and submit several documents for judicial notice in support of their contention that Petitioner was properly served notice. Whether Petitioner will be able to meet the relevant burden of proof on the factual allegation, or whether Respondents will possess sufficiently countervailing evidence, is not the issue on a demurrer. A ruling on a demurrer does not determine contested factual issues relating to the merits. Respondents' demurrer on the basis that the Amended Petition is untimely under Probate Code sections 16061.7 and 16061.8 is OVERRULED. In the alternative, Respondents demur to each cause of action pleaded in the Amended Petition on the basis that Petitioner fails to plead "ultimate facts" in relation to the causes of action, pleading only legal conclusions and statements "on information and belief." California Code of Civil Procedure section 425.10(a) establishes the basic pleading requirement that a complaint must contain a statement of the facts constituting the cause of action, in ordinary and concise language. Pleadings must contain "ultimate facts," rather than evidentiary facts or conclusions of law, to survive a demurrer under Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e). The "ultimate facts" pleading standard requires factual particularity in pleading the essential elements of a legal claim, sufficient to acquaint a defendant with the nature, source and extent of the cause of action, but does not require the plaintiff to plead the specific evidence he will use to prove those elements. (Doe v. City of Los Angeles (2007) 42 Cal.4th 531, 550.) Further, the doctrine of less particularity applies when defendants have superior knowledge of relevant facts (see, Ibid, and Thomas v. Regents of Univ. of California (2023) 97 Cal. App. 5th 587, 611) "Ultimate facts" are the logical conclusions deduced from evidentiary facts that establish the essential elements of a cause of action. (Rhode v. Bartholomew (1949) 94 Cal.App.2d 272). The courts have acknowledged the distinction between ultimate facts and legal conclusions is "not at all clear" and involves at most a matter of degree. (Burks v. Poppy Const. Co. (1962) 57 Cal.2d 463, 473.)

rulings.Case Title: ESTATE OF VICTORIA L. BRUGNARA
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For Purposes Of Posting Tentative Ruling.
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] The Demurrer specifically cites to Petitioner's allegations made on information and belief as insufficient to withstand demurrer for failure to state a cause of action, under Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e). The California Supreme Court in Doe v. City of Los Angeles (2007) 42 Cal. 4th 531, 550 established that a plaintiff may allege on information and belief any matters that are not within his personal knowledge, if he has information leading him to believe that the allegations are true. More recently, in Gomes v. Countrywide Home Loans (2011) 192 Cal. App. 4th 1149, 1159, the Court of Appeal held, "Because Gomes has conceded that he has no specific information about assignments of the Note, he would not be able to plead [the challenged allegations] on information and belief, based on facts leading him to believe they were true[.]" (emphasis in original.) Here, unlike the plaintiff in Gomes, Petitioner has not stated he would be unable to specify the facts leading him to believe as true the allegations he pleads on information and belief. Read as a whole, apart from the statements made on information and belief, the Amended Petition states reasons Petitioner believes what he pleads on information and belief is true. The causes of action in the Amended Petition are pleaded with sufficient "ultimate facts" to survive demurrer under Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e). This is evidenced by the substantive responses to the Amended Petition's claims and causes of action, presented in the Demurrer, supporting declarations and requests for judicial notice. Demurrer to each cause of action pleaded in the Amended Petition on the basis of failure to plead a cause of action per Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e) is OVERRULED. Petitioner shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.
